blurb: A rundown of the psychological and physiological research on gratitude practice — how consistently naming what we're thankful for measurably improves mood, sleep, and relationships.

title_clean: The Journey from Wellness to Wholeness
blurb: An interview with Elise Loehnen exploring why chasing 'wellness' can become its own trap, and what it means to pursue wholeness — integrating rather than optimizing away the messier parts of being human.

blurb: A science-journalism piece unpacking the research on affectionate touch — showing how something as simple as a hug or hand-hold can measurably lower anxiety and stress.

title_clean: Time Spent in Nature Can Boost Physical and Mental Well-Being
blurb: A Harvard public-health researcher summarizes evidence that regular time outdoors measurably improves both physical health and mental well-being.
A look at the cognitive science behind why we forget our own goals, and practical 'cognitive offloading' strategies — like reminders and environmental cues — that make follow-through easier.
A guide, drawing on expert input, to building social skills and easing the friction of connecting with others for anyone who wants to be more social.
A short video explainer on ASMR — the tingling, calming sensation some people experience from gentle sounds and whispers — and why it's become a popular relaxation practice.

blurb: An NPR roundup of reader-submitted strategies for holding onto optimism during difficult stretches, drawn from its Goats and Soda global-health and development beat.

title_clean: What It Felt Like to Show Myself Physical Affection for a Week
blurb: A first-person essay documenting a week-long experiment in self-directed physical affection — hugging, holding, and touching oneself — as a practice for easing loneliness and self-criticism.

title_clean: Lin-Manuel Miranda's Mom on Being an Abuela to Infants: Why Abuelas Are the True Guardians of Their Loved Ones' Mental Health
blurb: An essay by Luz Towns-Miranda on the singular role grandmothers — abuelas — play in Latino families as emotional anchors and mental-health guardians across generations.
A Washington Post interactive exploring the research on how birdsong measurably lowers stress and improves mood, making the case for nature's soundscape as a mental-health resource.
A look at the resurgence of African-rooted spiritual and wellness practices among Afro-Latinas, tracing how the 'bruja' trend reconnects a diaspora to ancestral healing traditions.

title_clean: Are People Generous When the Financial Stakes Are High?
blurb: An academic study testing whether generosity holds up when the money on the table gets bigger, challenging assumptions that prosocial behavior is only easy when stakes are low.

A PNAS study finding that redistributing wealth — not just accumulating it — increases happiness for givers and receivers alike, with implications for how societies think about inequality and well-being.
